data.description:
Add a first-class delete (hard-removal) command. Today the only removal is archive (ticket remains in the store); there is no documented way to purge a test/mistake ticket. Cleanup during the functionality audit required raw git rm on the tickets branch (finding #8).

DESIGN CONSTRAINT — deletion must have FRICTION and require EXPLICIT INTENT (this is destructive and irreversible against an append-only, auto-pushed store):
- Not a silent one-shot. Require an explicit confirmation token, e.g. `rebar delete <id> --yes-delete <id>` (must re-type the id) or an interactive y/N prompt with a `--force`/`--confirm` bypass for non-interactive use.
- Consider refusing to delete tickets that are linked-to by others (dangling refs) unless --force, and reporting what will break.
- Decide + document the store semantics: does delete write a TOMBSTONE event (preferred, replay-consistent, converges across clones) or physically remove the event directory? Append-only design (I1) favors a tombstone the reducer honors, not rm.
- Must work across all three interfaces (CLI/library/MCP) and respect REBAR_MCP_READONLY (write-gated). MCP exposure should be especially guarded.
- Update docs (README, CLAUDE.md, docs/event-schema.md if a TOMBSTONE event type is added) and add tests.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] delete requires explicit intent (re-typed id or confirm flag); a bare `delete <id>` with no confirmation does NOT delete
- [ ] deletion is reflected in list/show/fsck consistently and converges across clones (tombstone semantics decided + documented)
- [ ] dangling-reference handling defined (refuse or --force with warning)
- [ ] available + gated correctly across CLI, library, MCP (readonly blocks it)
- [ ] docs + tests added
data.title: Add a delete command with friction / explicit-intent guard
